questions.question: Explain the greedy choice property that makes Kruskal's Algorithm work. State the Cut Property.
keyPoints: Greedy approach: always choose smallest weight edge that doesn't form cycle
Cut Property: For any cut, the minimum weight edge crossing it belongs to some MST
Algorithm repeatedly applies this property by considering edges in increasing order

questions.question: Describe the Union-Find data structure used in Kruskal's Algorithm. Explain FIND and UNION operations.
keyPoints: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) maintains collection of disjoint sets
FIND operation returns the representative/root of the set containing a vertex
UNION operation merges two sets into one
Path compression and union by rank optimize performance

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Kruskal's Algorithm? Explain each component of the complexity.
keyPoints: Sorting edges: O(E log E) - dominant factor
Union-Find operations: O(E α(V)) - nearly constant amortized
Overall: O(E log E) or O(E log V)
Space complexity: O(V + E)

questions.question: Differentiate between Kruskal's Algorithm and Prim's Algorithm for finding MST.
keyPoints: Kruskal's: edge-based, starts with individual vertices, better for sparse graphs
Prim's: vertex-based, starts from a single vertex, better for dense graphs
Both produce MST but use different approaches
Kruskal's uses Union-Find; Prim's uses priority queue

questions.question: What happens when Kruskal's Algorithm is applied to a disconnected graph? Explain with reason.
keyPoints: Algorithm produces Minimum Spanning Forest instead of MST
One MST is generated for each connected component
Process continues until all edges are processed or each component has (Vi-1) edges
Useful in network design for connecting separate clusters

questions.question: Apply Kruskal's Algorithm step by step to find MST of the following graph with vertices {A,B,C,D,E} and edges: AB(1), BC(2), AC(3), CD(3), DE(4), BD(5), CE(6). Show the order of edge selection.
keyPoints: Sort edges: AB(1), BC(2), AC(3), CD(3), DE(4), BD(5), CE(6)
Select AB(1): no cycle
Select BC(2): no cycle, connects A-B-C
Select AC(3): would create cycle A-B-C-A, SKIP
Select CD(3): no cycle, adds D
Select DE(4): no cycle, adds E
Total edges: 4 (V-1=4), MST weight: 1+2+3+4=10

questions.question: State two real-world applications of Kruskal's Algorithm and explain why MST is suitable for these applications.
keyPoints: Application 1: Telephone/communication network design - connect all cities with minimum cable length
Application 2: Road/railway network planning - connect all places with minimum construction cost
Cluster analysis in data science
Image segmentation in computer vision
MST ensures global connectivity at minimum cost/weight

questions.question: Trace through Kruskal's Algorithm on the following weighted graph. Show all steps including the Union-Find operations and explain how cycle detection works. Graph: vertices {1,2,3,4,5,6} with edges: (1-2:3), (1-3:4), (2-3:5), (2-4:6), (3-4:7), (3-5:8), (4-5:9), (5-6:2), (4-6:1)
keyPoints: Initialize: each vertex in separate set
Edge 4-6(1): FIND(4)≠FIND(6), UNION, select edge
Edge 5-6(2): FIND(5)≠FIND(6), UNION, select edge
Edge 1-2(3): FIND(1)≠FIND(2), UNION, select edge
Edge 1-3(4): FIND(1)≠FIND(3), UNION, select edge
Edge 2-3(5): FIND(2)=FIND(3), cycle detected, SKIP
Edge 2-4(6): FIND(2)≠FIND(4), UNION, select edge - MST complete with 5 edges
Total weight: 1+2+3+4+6=16
Explain Union-Find state at each step

questions.question: Prove that Kruskal's Algorithm always produces a Minimum Spanning Tree. Use the Cut Property in your proof.
keyPoints: State Cut Property: For any cut, minimum crossing edge belongs to some MST
Base case: After first edge, property holds trivially
Inductive step: Assume after k edges, we have a partial forest that can be extended to MST
Consider next edge e selected by algorithm (minimum among non-cycle edges)
Consider cut separating vertices of e's components
By Cut Property, there exists MST containing e OR minimum crossing edge
If MST already contains e, done; otherwise can replace heavier edge
Show replacement doesn't increase total weight
Algorithm produces optimal MST by induction

questions.question: Write an algorithm for Kruskal's Algorithm in pseudocode. Also write the Union-Find operations with path compression and union by rank. Analyze the time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Pseudocode showing: sort edges, initialize Union-Find, iterate edges, use FIND for cycle detection, UNION to merge, stop at V-1 edges
Union-Find MAKE-SET, FIND with path compression, UNION by rank
Time: O(E log E) dominated by sorting
Space: O(V + E) for edges and Union-Find structure
Explain optimization benefits of path compression and union by rank
Mention practically constant amortized time O(α(n)) for Union-Find

questions.question: Compare Kruskal's Algorithm and Prim's Algorithm for finding Minimum Spanning Tree. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other? Provide examples of suitable graph types for each.
keyPoints: Comparison points: starting approach, data structures used, time complexity variations
Kruskal's: O(E log E), edge-based, Union-Find, better for sparse graphs
Prim's: O(E + V log V) with Fibonacci heap, vertex-based, better for dense graphs
Choose Kruskal's when: graph has many vertices but few edges, edges are already sorted or easily sortable
Choose Prim's when: graph is dense, need to start from specific vertex, graph represented as adjacency matrix
Example: For V=1000, E=5000 (sparse), prefer Kruskal's; For V=100, E=4500 (dense), prefer Prim's
